claim: Stripping Hand A's Layer-2 outers {y, n, r, ol, l} (mapped to Latin scribal abbreviations by CAPPELLI-MATCH-01) from Hand A tokens leaves a stem inventory with phonotactic properties compatible with Basque. Specifically: rare initial-r, balanced 5-vowel distribution, vowel-dominant word-final positions, low consonant-cluster density, and 35-45% overall vowel fraction.
evidence_for: CAPPELLI-MATCH-01 (2026-04-17) CONFIRMED 4/5 Layer-2 outers match documented Latin scribal abbreviation conventions structurally.
AGGLUTINATIVE-SCREEN-01 (2026-04-17) identified Basque as the best 5/5 morphological-profile match among non-harmonic agglutinative candidates.

If the CAPPELLI + AGGLUTINATIVE convergence reflects a real mechanism (Basque-like language written with Latin-style scribal abbreviation), then stripping the abbreviation layer should expose an underlying stem inventory with Basque-compatible phonotactics.

Basque has distinctive phonotactic features that distinguish it from Latin and from most other European languages (no initial r, strong CV-preference, 5-vowel balance, characteristic palatal and affricate consonants) — making phonotactic testing a concrete falsifier of the Basque-like hypothesis.
evidence_against: Stripping Layer-2 outers may not be sufficient — if Layer-1 inners are also part of the abbreviation layer (e.g., encoding theme vowels or additional inflection), the stripped 'stem' is still morphologically decorated and not directly comparable to a raw Basque lexical stem.

Basque phonotactic properties vary by historical period, dialect, and loanword integration. Using modern-Basque phonotactic ranges risks mismatch with 15th-c. Basque, for which limited attestation exists.

Hand A's glyph inventory includes items (e.g., ch, sh, ckh, cth, cph as ligatures) whose phonological mapping to Basque phonemes is entirely speculative without decoding. The phonotactic test treats EVA glyphs as category-assigned (vowel/consonant) based on Brain-V's sub-slot analysis, not based on a true phonological reading.
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE.

  DATA: Hand A tokens under Tokenisation A (MULTI_GLYPHS + 'ol'), N>=3 glyph-units.

  STRIPPING PROCEDURE:
    For each Hand-A token, remove the last glyph-unit IF it is in the Layer-2 outer set {y, n, r, ol, l}. Otherwise keep the token as-is. Additionally, remove leading 'qo' if the token begins with qo (per CAPPELLI-MATCH-01's word-initial qu- mapping). The remaining token is the STRIPPED STEM.

  GLYPH PHONOLOGICAL CATEGORIES (LOCKED):
    VOWEL-like: {i, e, o, a}
    CONSONANT-like: {k, t, p, d, l, m, n, r, s, q, f, g, v, x, y} plus ligatures {ch, sh, ckh, cth, cph, ol}
    (y, n, r, ol, l are stripped from Layer-2 position but may still appear medially in stems)

  FIVE PHONOTACTIC PROPERTIES (LOCKED):

  P1 — INITIAL-R RARITY:
    Compute fraction of stripped stems whose first glyph-unit is 'r'.
    Basque prediction: native Basque has no initial /r/; expect <5%.
    PASS if fraction <= 0.05.

  P2 — 5-VOWEL BALANCE:
    For the 4 vowel-like glyphs {i, e, o, a}, each must appear as a glyph-unit in >=5% of stripped stems. (Basque has a 5-vowel system with u as the 5th; EVA lacks a distinct u, so the test is for 4-vowel presence.)
    PASS if all of {i, e, o, a} are in at least 5% of stripped stems each.

  P3 — VOWEL-CONSONANT RATIO:
    Compute the fraction of glyph-units across all stripped stems that are vowel-like.
    Basque prediction: ~35-45% (Basque has vowel-heavy text).
    PASS if fraction in [0.35, 0.45].

  P4 — VOWEL-FINAL PREFERENCE:
    Compute fraction of stripped stems whose final glyph-unit is vowel-like.
    Basque prediction: >=40% (definite article -a, frequent vowel endings).
    PASS if fraction >= 0.40.

  P5 — LOW CONSONANT-CLUSTER DENSITY:
    Compute fraction of stripped stems that contain 2+ consecutive consonant-like glyph-units (i.e., a 2+ consonant cluster anywhere in the stem, not counting ligatures as clusters).
    Basque prediction: relatively low; most Basque native words follow CV patterns. Expect <25% of stems to have consonant clusters.
    PASS if fraction <= 0.25.

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    CONFIRMED: >=3/5 phonotactic properties PASS. Stripped stems are Basque-compatible.
    MARGINAL: 1-2/5 PASS. Partial compatibility; could be another language or the stripping is imperfect.
    REFUTED: 0/5 PASS. The Basque-like hypothesis fails at the phonotactic level.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED if >=3/5 pass. MARGINAL if 1-2/5. REFUTED if 0/5.

tests_run.details:
CONFIRMED 3/5 under locked rule — just at the threshold. 8779 stripped stems analysed (from 8794 Hand A N>=3 tokens after stripping Layer-2 outers and leading qo-).

  RESULTS:
    P1 Initial-r rarity:      24/8779 = 0.0027  PASS (<=0.05 threshold; sharpest Basque prediction passes decisively)
    P2 4-vowel balance:       i=0.223, e=0.301, o=0.498, a=0.372  PASS (all >=0.05)
    P3 V:C ratio:             0.4933  FAIL  (threshold [0.35, 0.45] — stems are MORE vowel-heavy than Basque)
    P4 Vowel-final:           0.6210  PASS  (threshold >=0.40; strong pass)
    P5 Consonant-cluster:     0.3610  FAIL  (threshold <=0.25; clusters are more common than Basque)

  INTERPRETATION:
  The sharpest single Basque-specific falsifier (P1 — absence of initial r in native Basque vocabulary) PASSES convincingly at 0.27% r-initial (well below the 5% threshold). This is a hard phonotactic rule that distinguishes Basque from Latin, Italian, Greek, Arabic, and most other European languages — Hand A's stripped stems respect it cleanly.

  P2 (4-vowel balance) and P4 (vowel-final preference) also pass, each supporting Basque compatibility. Stripped stems end in a vowel-like glyph 62% of the time, consistent with Basque's -a definite article and vowel-terminal lexical tendencies.

  The two failures are directionally consistent with each other but somewhat at odds with Basque:
  - P3 (V:C ratio 0.49) indicates stripped stems are MORE vowel-heavy than Basque, not less. This is the opposite direction of what would happen if stripping was insufficient (if we hadn't stripped enough consonant morphology, we'd see too few vowels).
  - P5 (cluster density 0.36) indicates more consonant clusters than Basque allows. This points to phonological structure unlike standard Basque.

  Together, P3 and P5 suggest the stripped stems have phonotactics that are VOWEL-HEAVY but also CLUSTER-HEAVY — an unusual combination. Possible explanations:
    (a) Stripping Layer-2 outers is correct but Layer-1 inners are ALSO part of the abbreviation layer, and real Basque stems are shorter/simpler than what remains.
    (b) Hand A encodes a language with Basque-like phonotactic rules (no initial r) but different overall V:C balance — perhaps a related but distinct Vasconic variant, or a non-Basque agglutinative language.
    (c) EVA glyph categorisation (vowel vs consonant) does not perfectly match the underlying phonology; some glyphs we classify as vowel-like may encode consonants or vice versa in the real writing system.

  The PASS verdict under the locked rule is real — the strongest single test (r-initial) is decisive. But the partial failure on V:C balance and cluster density means the match is not clean. The Basque-like hypothesis is supported but not confirmed in full.

  DIAGNOSTIC: top-10 stem-initial glyphs — ch (19%), o (17%), d (16%), k (11%), sh (8%), t (7%), y (6%), s (3%), cth (3%), a (2%). Dominated by consonant-like ligatures and single stops. Interestingly, 'y' as stem-initial (6%) is non-trivial — either stems really can start with y, or some tokens had y-final stripped twice (but the stripping is single-pass, so 'y' here is a truly medial or internal y).

verdict_note: CONFIRMED 3/5 — exactly at threshold. The sharpest Basque-specific test passes: 0.27% r-initial, far below the 5% threshold. 4-vowel balance and vowel-final preference also pass. The two failures (V:C ratio 0.49 > 0.45, cluster density 0.36 > 0.25) indicate stems are simultaneously MORE vowel-heavy AND MORE cluster-heavy than standard Basque — an unusual combination suggesting either incomplete stripping, a Basque-adjacent but non-Basque language, or EVA glyph-phonology miscategorisation. The Basque-like hypothesis is supported on the hardest falsifier but shows quantitative divergence on V:C composition metrics.
findings_for_followup: The r-initial rate of 0.27% is a strong positive signal. Only 24 stripped stems begin with r across 8779 — consistent with a language that categorically forbids initial r. Basque, Armenian, and a few other languages share this constraint. Follow-up: check if these 24 r-initial stems are loanwords (e.g., from Latin) — Basque loanwords commonly add an epenthetic vowel to avoid r-initial (e.g., Latin rosa > Basque arrosa). If Hand A's r-initial stems are rare and isolated (not part of productive paradigms), the Basque reading is further supported.

The V:C ratio failure (0.49 vs 0.45 ceiling) is small — 4 percentage points above Basque range. If Layer-1 inners are ALSO abbreviation marks (e.g., theme vowels from Latin declension), additional stripping could bring V:C down. A follow-up test: strip BOTH Layer-1 inner and Layer-2 outer, then re-run P3 and P5.

The cluster density of 36% is too high for Basque. Inspect WHICH consonant pairs are forming clusters — if they are all (ligature + single consonant) pairs like ch+d, sh+k, that might reflect a phonological reality (ligatures are heavy single consonants, not clusters). Reclassifying ligatures as single phonemes would reduce the apparent cluster rate.

24 r-initial stems is small enough to inspect individually. If any are plausible Basque words (or Basque-compatible stems), that would be diagnostic. Candidate inspection: e.g., if one is 'ri', 'ren', 'rik' — these are Basque suffixes, not stems; their appearance as 'stems' would indicate the stripping missed a layer.
reasoning: The CAPPELLI-MATCH-01 + AGGLUTINATIVE-SCREEN-01 convergence identified a hypothesis (Basque-like agglutinative language under Latin scribal abbreviation), not a finding. If the hypothesis is correct, stripping the Latin-abbreviation layer (Layer-2 outers) should reveal a stem inventory with Basque-specific phonotactic signatures — particularly the characteristic absence of initial /r/ in native Basque vocabulary, which is a hard phonotactic rule not shared by Latin, Italian, or most European languages. This is the sharpest single falsifier available: if stripped Hand A stems are r-initial at more than 5%, the Basque-like hypothesis is in serious trouble. The other four properties provide supporting evidence.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed falsification attempt on Basque-like+Latin-abbreviation convergence hypothesis; test 1 of 4
